coredata.dc:title: Carbon Nano-Dots based potentiometric sensor for the assay of the antiviral agent; Valacyclovir: In-Depth DFT Calculations, application to the pharmaceutical formulation and greenness assessment

coredata.dc:description: Valacyclovir (VAL), as a prodrug to acyclovir antiviral agent, has recently held a lot of interest for its efficacy boosting owing to the significant improvement in bioavailability. This makes it a successful candidate in the management of herpes simplex strains as well as CORONA viruses. In this work a carbon nano dot-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) has been optimized as potentiometric sensor for the assay of VAL through a systematic approach. A mixture components of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) membrane have been selected and fine-tuned for optimum sensitivity and selectivity. The best results have been obtained by drop casting a blend in % w/w of PVC; 30.85, tricrasyl phosphate (TCP) as plasticizer; 64.32, phosphotungstic acid (PT) as cation exchanger; 1.61 and phosphorus – nitrogen-doped carbon nano-dots (PNCDs); 3.22 over the GCE. The density function theory (DFT) calculations have been applied to the optimized structures of VAL and the CDs and have proven high binding affinity of 46 kcal/mol dominated by π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding. The developed sensor has exhibited a swift response time of six seconds at a 5.00 pH value. The ICH guidelines have been applied to validate the optimized parameters. A good linearity range has been obtained from 1.18 × 10-3 down to 1.07 × 10-6 M for a correlation coefficient 0.9996 with a Nernstian slope of 33.16 mV/decade. The greenness has been assessed using the AGREE approach, giving a score of 0.8, which has indicated the environmental friendliness. An average % recovery of almost 99.00 % was obtained by application to the tablet formulations and furtherly it has been monitored by the standard addition method, giving recoveries varied from 98.00 and up to 102.00 % for relative standard deviation values (%RSD) less than 1.50 %, evidencing efficiency for application in the quality control laboratories.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Valacyclovir (VAL), as a prodrug to acyclovir antiviral agent, has recently held a lot of interest for its efficacy boosting owing to the significant improvement in bioavailability. This makes it a successful candidate in the management of herpes simplex strains as well as CORONA viruses. In this work a carbon nano dot-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) has been optimized as potentiometric sensor for the assay of VAL through a systematic approach. A mixture components of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) membrane have been selected and fine-tuned for optimum sensitivity and selectivity. The best results have been obtained by drop casting a blend in % w/w of PVC; 30.85, tricrasyl phosphate (TCP) as plasticizer; 64.32, phosphotungstic acid (PT) as cation exchanger; 1.61 and phosphorus – nitrogen-doped carbon nano-dots (PNCDs); 3.22 over the GCE. The density function theory (DFT) calculations have been applied to the optimized structures of VAL and the CDs and have proven high binding affinity of 46 kcal/mol dominated by π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding. The developed sensor has exhibited a swift response time of six seconds at a 5.00 pH value. The ICH guidelines have been applied to validate the optimized parameters. A good linearity range has been obtained from 1.18 × 10-3 down to 1.07 × 10-6 M for a correlation coefficient 0.9996 with a Nernstian slope of 33.16 mV/decade. The greenness has been assessed using the AGREE approach, giving a score of 0.8, which has indicated the environmental friendliness. An average % recovery of almost 99.00 % was obtained by application to the tablet formulations and furtherly it has been monitored by the standard addition method, giving recoveries varied from 98.00 and up to 102.00 % for relative standard deviation values (%RSD) less than 1.50 %, evidencing efficiency for application in the quality control laboratories.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Valacyclovir is the L-valyl ester derivative of the antiviral agent acyclovir (ACV). Upon oral administration, VAL undergoes extensive first-pass metabolism to produce acyclovir [1]. One major advantage of VAL is its significantly improved oral absorption and prolonged plasma concentration levels compared to ACV [2,3]. These properties contribute to a three to five-fold increase in the bioavailability of VAL, making it a more potent antiviral agent [4]. Mainly, VAL is utilized for the treatment and prevention of various herpes simplex (HSV) families like HSV-1 and HSV-2 in addition to varicella-zoster (VZV) viral infections [5,6]. Valacyclovir is highly effective for preventing herpes eye infections and requires only half the ACV dose while showing a lower incidence of resistant viral strains [7]. Recently, it has been introduced as a potential solution to the global COVID-19 pandemic in accordance to promising pre-clinical studies [8]. Chemically, VAL is 2 − [ (2 – amino – 6 – oxo − 1H – purin – 9 – yl) methoxy ] ethyl (2S) – 2 −amino – 3 – methyl butanoate hydrochloride (Fig. 1 ). A liquid chromatographic analysis with mass–mass detection was employed for the determination of VAL alone [9], simultaneously with its metabolites [10,11] and simultaneously with ACV [12] in human plasma samples. Liquid chromatography has been furtherly exploited with UV detection for determining VAL in pharmaceuticals and human plasma [13,14] as well as the enantiomeric separation of its active l-form at 254 nm [15]. Simple UV spectrophotometric analysis has been used to assess VAL at 251 nm [16]. The interaction between fluorescamine and VAL in a basic medium results in a highly fluorescent derivative at an emission wavelength of 475 nm, which was utilized to determine VAL in spiked plasma samples [17]. A specially designed electrode has been fabricated for the selective determination of VAL in the presence of its acid degradation product, guanine [18]. A highly sensitive flow-injection amperometric method has been developed to determine VAL in pharmaceuticals on carbositall electrodes with a preactivated surface [19]. The square-wave and differential pulse voltammetry technique have been used to analyze VAL in pharmaceuticals [20]. A molecularly imprinted modified carbon paste electrode has been exploited for selective determination of VAL using cyclic voltammetry [21]. A liquid contact PVC potentiometric sensor was fabricated for assay VAL in presence of its acid induced degradation product using sodium tetraphenyl borate (TPB) as a cation exchanger and bis (2-ethylhexyl) sebacate as

a plasticizer but this developed sensor has shown limited quantitation limit of 1.00 × 10-5 M due to the cons of the liquid contact sensors [18,22]. It is clearly noticeable that, all the previously reported methods require expensive chromatographic, spectroscopic, and electrochemical instrumentation and many pretreatment steps before VAL monitoring. The potentiometric measurements using solid contact (SC) sensors have exhibited great potential in eco-friendly analysis. They have overcome the previous drawbacks by providing extreme simplicity with minimized sampling demand, minimum space, cost-effectiveness, high speed, broad dynamic range, and susceptibility to miniaturization. Herein, they could convincingly afford real-time potential monitoring by positioning as unique in-situ interfaces [23,24]. The operation of these potentiometric sensors is dependent on the assessment of the difference in potential between the GCE as working electrode and the Ag-AgCl as reference electrode, which is being directly linked to the logarithm of VAL molar concentration, and described by the Nernst equation [25]. Among favors of the solid contact sensors that they surpass the reported liquid contact sensor [18] in sensitivity because of limitation of the transmembrane flux of the parent ion in the sample solution and boasting a simpler construction by relying on solid support like a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) [22]. The nano-carbon quantum dots (CDs) are quasi-0D carbon-based particles possessing sp2 conjugated cores. They have been chosen as participant members in the electrode surface modification mixture because of their favorable attributes. They have many advantages as potential transducers [25,26] having small sizes down to 20.00 nm diameter, consequently, they have high sensitivity with insulator layers of high input impedance avoiding the chemical equilibrium disturbance of the electrolytes. Hence, they become able to measure the potential changes with respect to electrolyte/insulator interface [27–29]. Additionally, they have excellent optical criteria, good photostability, eco-friendliness, abundant functional moieties (i.e., carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl), tunable fluorescence, and photo-induced electron transfer ability [30–32]. The nano-carbon quantum dots (CDs) offer several merits when employed as a modifier for solid contact sensors, including facile synthesis and low-cost production, simple functionalization routes, high sensitivity, excellent stability, and biocompatibility, making them a prospective material for sensor modification in the electrochemical applications [33,34]. They have been employed as modifiers of SC sensors in several applications to enhance the transduction process, for instance, the developed CDs based biosensor to monitor phosphate and glucose [35], ultrasensitive detection of dopamine in plasma [34], and monitoring of the organophosphorus pesticide; malathion in water [36]. The GCE is considered a highly suitable choice as SC electrode for the electroanalysis due to its excellent electrical conductivity, low reactivity, and non-invasiveness to samples with small volumes. Consequently, many electrochemical methods have utilized it as a support for ISE [37,38]. Additionally, the modification of GCE with CDs has shown significant enhancement of their electrochemical properties, leading to a marked decrease in overvoltage and reduced charge transfer resistance, thereby, improving performances in the electrochemical sensing applications [39]. Furthermore, the intrinsic hydrophobicity of CDs prevents or diminishes the growth of water layer at the GCE / membrane interface. The presence of this water layer disrupts the potential and reduces the sensitivity since it serves as a polar repository for the parent ion.[22,40]. In the current work, different kinds of CDs have been synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment and doped by different moieties and metal ions to improve either sensitivity, selectivity or both. Hence, these CDs were employed to develop and optimize the first carbon dots modified SC-ISE potentiometric sensor for direct and green assay of VAL content in pharmaceuticals. A systematic approach has been designed to evaluate the sensor components that control the performance of each fabricated sensor. The key factors that play vital roles in the performance of the membrane sensor have been investigated and namely; the plasticizer, the ion exchanger, the plasticizer to polyvinyl chloride (PVC) ratio, and the type of CDs modifier. The greenness profile of the proposed method has been assessed using the Analytical greenness metric (AGREE) calculator [41].

A Jenway pH/mV meter, model 3510 (Essex, UK), has been used for the potentiometric measurements with a Jenway pH glass electrode. Thermo scientific® double junction Ag/AgCl external reference electrode, model Orion 900,200 (Waltham, USA). The prepared sensors were built on the glassy carbon electrodes support purchased from CH Instruments (Austin, USA). Magnetic stirrer (Daihan-Korea) for the in-situ potentiometric measurements. A locally designed Teflon-lined stainless steel hydrothermal autoclave (50.00 mL) was manufactured per the standard dimensions. Nicolet 6700 FTIR Advanced Gold Spectrometer, supported with OMNIC 8 software (Thermo Electron Scientific Instruments Corp., Madison, WI USA) for data analysis. The hydraulic press was applied to obtain KBr sample discs using a Thermo-scientific (Fischer, USA) Qwik handi-press instrument and a Perkin Elmer die press. High-resolution electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEM-100-CXII plus JEOL microscope working at 120 kV). X-ray diffraction pattern (Philips PW-1700) was used with wavelength Cu-Kα radiation at 40 KV and 30 mA settings and a diffraction angle range (20°-70°) with a step of 0.06°.

Valacyclovir (99.80 % − pharmaceutical grade) has been kindly provided by Hikma Pharmaceuticals (6th October city, Giza, Egypt). For fabrication of the potentiometric sensors: analytical grade high molecular weight (10,000) polyvinyl chloride (PVC, 99.80 %) powder, sodium tetraphenylborate (TPB, > 99.50 %), phosphotungstic acid hydrate (PT, 99.80 %), phosphomolybdic acid hydrate (PM, ACS reagent, > 99.50 %), tri-cresyl phosphate (TCP, > 99.80 %), dibutyl phthalate (DBP, 99.00 %), dibutyl sebacate (DBS, > 97.00 %), dioctyl phthalate (DOP, > 99.50 %) and tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.60 %) have been purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Other chemicals used in this study are; analytical grade ethylenediamine (>99.00 %) and orthophosphoric acid (85.00 %) which were purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). Sodium hydroxide pellets (99.50 %), hydrochloric acid (37.00 %), and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (99.80 %) from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemical Company (Cairo, Egypt). All salts for the selectivity study like NaCl, KCl, MgSO4, ZnSO4, CuCl2, FeCl3, NH4Cl, Co(NO3)2, Pb(CH3COO)2, and CaCl2 (99.60 – 99.90 %) have been supplied from (El-Nasr pharmaceutical chemical company, Cairo, Egypt). Imported Valtrex 500 tablets (Glaxo Smithkline, USA) were purchased from the Egyptian market.

Into a 25.00 mL volumetric flask, 0.09 g of VAL was transferred, dissolved in a suitable volume of phosphate buffer solution adjusted to pH 5, and completed to the mark with the same solution to get a 0.01 M solution. Further dilutions have been applied to obtain a concentrations down to 1.00 × 10-6 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Pulp-free orange juice has been obtained after chopping and squeezing fresh oranges. The different mixtures of the four kinds of CDs have been synthesized in a hydrothermal autoclave under the same conditions of 200.00 °C for six hours. The nitrogen-doped nano-carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared by introducing 25.00 mL of fresh orange juice into the Teflon-lined cylinder. Then, 0.50 mL of ethylenediamine was added slowly, drop by drop, to the cylinder's edge with careful glass rod stirring. Phosphorus-–nitrogen-doped nano-carbon dots (PNCDs), have been prepared similarly in addition to providing 2.00 mL H3PO4 after ten minutes of ethylenediamine supplying with a very cautious manner and dropwise addition at the cylinder edge with slow glass rod stirring. Metal-doped nano-carbon dots were prepared by mixing the same volumes of fresh orange juice with 150.00 mg amounts of CuSO4 and ZnSO4, followed by fast stirring to ensure the dissolution of salts. The resultant solutions were allowed to cool at room temperature overnight. Afterwards, these dark brown mixtures were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatants were ultra-filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filters. The solutions were then introduced into dialysis membranes and stirred in one litre of deionized water that was changed every hour during the day until clear and colourless solutions were obtained. Finally, the solutions within the dialysis membrane were lyophilized to get the different solid nanoparticles. The obtained CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR), The transmission electron microscope (TEM), and the X-Ray Powder Diffraction (XRPD).

Various membrane cocktails have been prepared according to the details presented in (Table 1 ). Precise mixtures have been achieved of predetermined quantities of three crucial components controlling the sensor performance: PVC, plasticizer (TCP, DBP, or DOP), and ion exchanger (TPB, PT, or PM). These blends were then transferred into 5.00 mL volumetric flasks, followed by the addition of a suitable amount of THF to dissolve these mixtures. The volumes were then adjusted to the mark using the same solvent. Subsequently, accurately measured volumes of each membrane mixture were individually drop-casted onto the polished surface of the GCE and allowed to dry to get the sensors. The fabricated sensors have been preconditioned before use by soaking them in a 0.02 M standard VAL solution for one hour, then rinsed with deionized water and dried with soft tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of the GCE with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The study has offered two protocols to modify the GCE, as presented in (Table 1). The first protocol involved modification of the surface of the GCE by drop-casting 20.00 µL (5.00 mg mL−1) colloidal suspension of different CDs (NCDs, PNCDs, CuCDs, and ZnCDs) in water and left to dry for one hour before application of the membrane recipes as mentioned under section (2.5.2. composition of the sensor membrane), sensors (7 – 10), (Table 1). The second protocol entailed incorporation of the colloidal suspension of the studied CDs dispersed in THF as ionophore in the membrane recipes besides modification of the GCE surface by the PNCDs, sensors (11 – 14), (Table 1).

The potentiometric measurements have been conducted at room temperature to VAL standard solutions prepared in the concentration range of 1.00 × 10−2 to 1.00 × 10−7 M at phosphate buffer pH 5.00 (5.00 × 10-3 M). The developed sensors and the reference Ag/AgCl electrodewere immersed in their corresponding solutionsto measure the potential difference within a range of ± 1.00 mV. The calibration curves have been constructed by graphing the electromotive force (EMF) against the logarithm of the molar concentration. The stated values have been calculated as the mean of five measurements. The calibration graphs' linear segments were used to build regression equations for each sensor andto calculate their slopes and correlation coefficients. The optimal membrane has been chosen through a systematic evaluation, prioritizing the criteria of the highest Nernstian slope, followed by the highest correlation coefficient and the most comprehensive linearity range. This selection process thoroughly examined the most suitable cationic exchanger, the optimal plasticizer, and the most effective CDs as surface modifiers or ionophores.

An investigation has been carried out on the influence of the different pH values of 1.00 × 10−3 M VAL solution on the developed potential of the optimized sensor. The optimized sensor, the pH glass electrode, and the external Ag/AgCl reference electrodes were dipped in the VAL standard solution. Then, the pH was changed gradually to cover the range of (1.50 – 12.50). The pH and the potential have been recorded simultaneously to plot the pH-potential curve.

The optimized sensor's selectivity has been evaluated by calculating the potentiometric selectivity coefficients

using the separate solution method [42], as endorsed by the IUPAC guidelines [43]. The potential generated by a 1.00 × 10-3 M VAL (E VAL ) has been compared to that produced by equal concentrations of interfering species (E I ). The assessment encompassed the commonly encountered ionic pollutants in the studied matrix, such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, NH4 +, Zn2+and Co2+. The Nicolsky-Eisenman equation was applied in the analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct determination of VAL in pharmaceutical formulations
ce:section.ce:para:
A total of ten Valtrex 500® tablets, each containing 500.00 mg of VAL, were weighed and then finely pulverized using a mortar. An amount of the fine powder was accurately weighed and put into a 25.00 mL volumetric flask to be dissolved in a 5.00 ×10-3 M phosphate buffer at pH 5.00 to give concentration equivalent to 10-5 M VAL. The solution was then ultrasonicated for 30 min to improve the extraction process, followed by the standard addition technique [44]. Each solution's potential has been recorded before and after introducing 1.00 mL of 1.00 × 10−2 M VAL standard solution. The following equation (Equation (2) has been utilized to determine the molar VAL concentration Cx.
(2)

In this equation, Cx and Vx represent the molar concentration and volume of the sample solution, respectively, while Cs and Vs denote the molar concentration and volume of the added standard solution, respectively. ΔE is the measured potential difference due to the addition of a standard solution. S corresponds to the slope of the calibration curve, determined immediately before the analysis. The experiment was replicated six times, and an average percentage recovery was calculated. In addition, several increments have been added from (1.00 × 10-2 M) VAL standard solution to the tablet solution, and then the mean % recovery and RSD values were obtained.

Geometries of the functionalized PNCDs, VAL, and their complex were optimized using DFT with the B3LYP function and the D3BJ correction [45,46] as well as the basis set 6-31G(d). This dispersion correction is necessary for long-range and noncovalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking [47]. These are essential in this study and crucial for obtaining accurate intermolecular interactions and binding energies. Several conformers were considered for the ground state geometry, and the one with the lowest energy was selected. This global minimum was confirmed by harmonic vibrational frequency. We have considered several binding modes between the functionalized PNCDs and VAL, and then the most stable one was selected. The binding energy ΔEb for the complex was calculated at the same level of theory according to the relation: ΔEb = Egraph-val – (Egraph + Eval), (Equation 3). The solvent effect was included using the PCM continuum solvation model, with water as a solvent.

The development of rapid, simple, and inexpensive analytical methods without hazardous reagents and complicated instruments aligns with the principles of green analytical chemistry (GAC). Currently, this approach is of global interest to all researchers. The potentiometric ion-selective electrodes has afforded an ideal solution to meet these objectives by swiftly and effectively determining analytes in complex matrices, eliminating the necessity for preliminary preparation or derivatization steps [48]. Furthermore, enhancing solid contact sensors by incorporating novel nanomaterials such as CDs leverages the exceptional advantages of these materials. This is an additional supporting factor to augment the electrochemical performance of the newly developed sensors [22]. In this context, we developed and optimized the first CDs-modified solid contact potentiometric sensor to directly assay VAL in the pharmaceutical preparations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the selected PNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been proven by the careful investigation of the synthesized nano-dots that, the PNCDs have improved the electrochemical behaviour of the designed sensor. Consequently, it has been chosen for the detailed structural analysis study. The inspection of the FTIR spectrum of the PNCDs has revealed many vibrational features. Two broad stretching vibrations at 3382 and 3225 cm−1 corresponding to the hydroxyl group of the carboxylate moiety and the NH of the secondary amine, respectively. A stretching vibration of an aliphatic methyl moiety appeared at 2916 cm−1. The carbonyl group of the carboxylate moiety and a secondary amide have been presented by stretching vibrations at 1635 and 1554 cm−1, respectively. The vibrational features at 1435, 1338, 1038, and 929 cm−1 referred to P-C stretching (symmetric deformation), C-H bending, P-O stretching, and hydroxyl bending, respectively [49–51]. The TEM image has witnessed mono-spherical particles, which are almost unitizied with size distribution ranged in between 3.68 and 6.52 nm with an average diameter of 4.78 nm. A small percentage not more than 7.50 % have exhibited average particle size of 9.50 nm. Small aggregates have been observed which are attributed to evaporation of the solvent throughout the TEM examinations. As for the XRPD spectrum, a typical noisy and broad peak at 24.50° has emerged indicating 0.34 nm interlayer spacing (Fig. 2 ). This could result from high distortion of the carbon atoms by the doped heteroatoms confirming the formation of the CDs [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fabricated sensor membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The potentiometric response relied primarily on the existence of ion exchangers, plasticizers, and ionophores in the PVC membrane. The plasticized PVC matrix is an ideal option for the potentiometric sensing due to its network structure, which enables the unrestricted flow of ionic species within the membrane, hence increasing membrane conductance. [53]. We have devised a systematic model to evaluate various factors directly influencing the selectivity and sensitivity of the developed sensors. These factors encompass the types and proportions of ion exchangers and plasticizers and the incorporation of CDs as ion to electron transducers at the GCE/ membrane interface or as ionophores in the PVC membrane (Table 1). To get the desired electrode performance, one can customize the types of PVC membrane components and finely tune their concentrations. Furthermore, the use of the GCE assensor support is justified due to its advantageous characteristics, such as high conductivity, low background current, ample rigidity, and an expansive potential range [54]. The solid contact sensor provides an excellent substitute for the classical liquid contact sensor due to the ease of preparation and enhancing the sensitivity due to laying off the internal filling solution that might leak into the working solutions [18]. As presented in (Table 1). We have developed a total of fourteen membrane sensors, and their performance characteristics have been evaluated in terms of the slope, linear range, and correlation coefficient.

Valacyclovir is a basic drug (pKa = 7.80) and it behaves as divalent cation at the acidic pH, hence, the optimum Nernstian slope is considered to be close to the half numerical value of 59. Throughout the one factor at a time optimization (OFAT), pH 5.00 has experienced the closest value to the Nernstian behavior among whole the studied basic media (supposed to be ≈ 59) or acidic ones (supposed to be ≈ 30).

The fabricated sensors have exhibited a cationic response through the incorporation of the cation exchangers, which have introduced anionic sites within the membrane. These anionic sites play a crucial role in enhancing the perm-selectivity of the sensor (Donnan's exclusion) which minimize the interference from oppositely charged ions [55]. Consequently, the addition of the cation-exchanger to the PVC membrane facilitates the preferential transfer of VAL cations to the lipophilic phase, while excluding the counter ion. This process leads to the formation of a phase boundary potential due to the charge separation near the membrane-sample interface, resulting in a Nernstian response. The potential difference at this boundary is supposed to be directly proportional to the ionic activity of the VAL cations in the sample solution.

The conductivity of a plasticized membrane is primarily dependent on the mobility of ion exchanger ions. It is essential to choose the ion exchangers with appropriate lipophilicity to prevent leaching into the aqueous sample phase, which could adversely affect the lifetime of the sensors [56]. Additionally the ion exchangers promote the sensitivity of the developed sensors by decreasing the ion free energy which enhances the extraction of the target ions [57].

In that sense, we have tested three different cationic exchangers namely (PT, PM, and TPB) while using TCP as solvent mediator sensor (1 – 3), (Table 1). Sensor 1 containing PT demonstrated the best Nernstian slope compared to the other sensors, so we have used PT in the subsequent studies.

The basic component of the membrane sensor – represents ca. two thirds of the sensor – is the solvent mediator or the plasticizer. It serves to maintain the elasticity of the PVC membrane, preventing cracking, and to solubilize the lipophilic membrane components, ensuring the mobility of ions necessary for membrane conductivity. Therefore, leaching of the plasticizer deteriorates the membrane’s performance and significantly reduces its lifespan [56]. Four different plasticizers (TCP, DBP, DBS, and DOP) have been tested sensors (1 and 4 – 6) as witnessed in (Table 1). The sensors were checked concerning the Nernstian slope, linearity range, and correlation coefficient. The No. 1 sensor containing the TCP has been picked up for the further studies because it achieved the best performance characteristics.

The introduction of the nanomaterials like carbon dots has evidenced to significantly boost the advancement of SC-ISEs. They have gained attention as ion to electron transducers in the modification of the GCE and hence, provide positive impacts on the electron transfer and the redox interaction. They promote durability and miniaturization, making them apt for various applications and fabrication of sensors that operate without the need for continuous calibration. Moreover, they have low toxicity, large surface are and show high biocompatibility [58,59 60]. Testing of different modifiable ISEs in our study has clearly deduced that doping of the CDs with the heteroatoms has shown excellent impact in selective sensing capability of the cationic analytes since they provide high ionic stability and high probabilities of electrostatic and non-electrostatic interactions [61–64].

Despite the numerous benefits of SC-ISEs, they confront significant limits, such as issues with repeatability, membrane stability, and drifting in potential due to the continuous formation of an aqueous layer at the SC/membrane boundary [65]. This water layer behaves as reservoir for the parent ion leading to its diffusion in the diluted sample which negatively affects the sensitivity of the prepared sensors. In addition, excessive formation of this layer leads to detachment of the PVC membrane from the GCE surface which destroys the sensor. The providing of hydrophobic interlayer between the membrane and the GCE limits the formation of this aqueous layer [65]. The CDs exhibit hydrophobicity, with water contact angles (WCA) typically ranging from 108.9° to 110.34° [66]. The WCA is a measure of the interaction between a solid surface and water. A surface is considered hydrophobic if its WCA is greater than 90°, and superhydrophobic if its WCA is greater than 150° [67]. From this respective, four sensors (7 – 10), (Table 1) have been modified with different CDs; NCDs, PNCDs, CuCDs and ZnCDs by the drop casting technique. Generally, adoption of these CDs as signal transducer at the GCE/membrane interface stabilizes the potential, decreases the signal drifting, and widening the linear range, herein, the best performance was obtained with the PNCDs modified cocktail, sensor No. 10 as evident in (Table 1).

Eventually, the optimized sensor (Sensor No.14) was prepared by modifying the GCE surface with PNCDs then drop casting the PVC membrane which contains PT as cation exchanger, TCP as plasticizer, and finally supplying the PNCDs as ionophore. Notably, the optimized sensor demonstrated a Nernstian response (33.17 mv/decade) to VAL, achieving a remarkably low detection limit (7.88 × 10-7 mol/L), (Table 1 and Fig. 3 ).

A stable response has been obtained over the pH range (2.50 – 8.50) upon subjecting sensor No. 14 to the pH change in 1.00 × 10-3 M VAL solution. The potential was sharply decreased at pH higher than 8 due to formation of free VAL base while the presence of H3O+ at low pH values led to deterioration of the potential response (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic response time and lifetime of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As per IUPAC recommendations [43], the dynamic response time evaluated the time consumed to achieve the equilibrium potential and ensure the stability of the sensor's response. Thus, we have gradually increased the concentration of VAL solution and recorded the time required to reach stable responses. Sensor No. 14 has exhibited a fast response time of 6.00 ± 2 s within ± 1.00 mV potential response (Fig. 5 ). The sensor’s lifetime was tested by periodic repeating the calibration curve and determining the corresponding slopes. It was found that, sensor No. 14 has displayed a stable slope within ± 1.00 mV/ decade over 30 days (Table 1). Beyond this period a significant decline in the slope has been observed (Fig S1). During periods of inactivity, the sensor was kept in a 1.00 × 10−2 M VAL standard solution.

The selectivity is monitored by the separate solution method and hence getting the potentiometric selectivity coefficient (
K
Val , I
Pot
) that represents ratio in between the activity of the targeted ion and the other interfering ones that could exhibit identical potential response under the same conditions. By applying the Nikolsky–Eisenman equation (1), the tested cations have exhibited
K
Val , I
Pot
values much less than unity ranged from 6.60 × 10-7 up to 3.18 × 10-4 indicating the efficiency of the optimized sensor and the optimized analytical conditions in targeting VAL even in highly interfering media. Furthermore, the doping of membrane sensor with PNCDs enhances the detection of VAL cations rather than interfering species, (Table 2
).

The proposed method has been validated in accordance to the ICH guidelines [68]. A wide linearity range has been obtained from 1.18 × 10-3 − 1.07 × 10-6 M with excellent correlation coefficient of 0.9996 and almost optimum Nernstian slope of 33.16 mV/decade. Excellent sensitivity has been obtained with LOD value of 7.88 × 10-7M which was determined with compliance to IUPAC recommendations [43] at the cross mark obtained when calibration curve was extrapolated. The accuracy study has been achieved as five determinations on three concentrations within the linearity range representing the low, mid and the high levels. It was assessed as percentage recoveries with successful mean value of 99.00 %. The precision has been estimated as percentage relative standard deviation (% RSD) on two aspects. The first one is the repeatability as three measurements of the same concentration levels in different times within a day. The other aspect is the intermediate precision by performing the same measurements three consecutive days. Both studies have proven reliability of the current study with %RSD of less than 2.00 %, Table 3 . Furthermore, the developed sensor has shown an advantage of wider pH working ranges and wide linear range with a relatively low detection limit when compared to the reported liquid contact sensor [18] (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potentiometric determination of VAL in pharmaceutical formulations
ce:section.ce:para: The developed sensor (No.14) has been successfully employed for the direct determination of VAL in tablet suspension without pre-preparation steps like filtration or extraction. The obtained results proved satisfactory, evident from favorable % recovery and % RSD (Table 4 ). The accuracy of the proposed method has been furtherly assessed by the standard addition technique. It has been proven that, the added standard yielded commendable recoveries and % RSD values, underscoring the absence of interference from the background matrix in the potentiometric measurements (Table 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The DFT calculations on functionalized graphene with Valacyclovir

ce:section.ce:para: An exceptional potentiometric sensor has been introduced for the determination of valacyclovir. An extensive optimization has been operated to the composition of the selective electrochemical detector, the pH of the medium and the dynamic response. A unique blend of provided mixture components including high molecular PVC, TCP, and PT as percentages by weight has experienced promising results. It was found that the introduction of nitrogen and phosphorus doped carbon nano-dots into the formulated mixture has greatly improved the electrochemical behavior. The positive impact of the CDs has been verified through a comprehensive DFT calculations what have shown strong binding interactions to the VAL molecule. An ICH based validation investigation has witnessed excellent performance with respect to wide linearity range, correlation coefficient, high sensitivity, high accuracy and proven precision with %RSD values less than 2.00 %. The developed electrode has evidenced high selectivity to the potential interfering cations. The method has been successfully applied to tablet formulations with excellent recovery percentages confirming its suitability for quality control laboratories. In terms of the eco-friendly metrics, the proposed method has proved to be extremely green according to AGREE approach what is based on the 12 principles of GAC.
